grounding_note: Row 252 (divorce/annulment with Navy husband) split humans between plain Divorce and Family Law > Military; the models strongly supported Military. A spouse's active-duty status, an existing decree needing modification, or a marriage that may qualify for annulment are institutional facts users hold but bury inside 'I need a divorce.'

scenarios.opening_query: I need to file for divorce. We've been married four years and there's no fixing this.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || Family Law > Military
scenarios.hidden_fact: My husband is active-duty Army, currently deployed overseas.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He's active-duty Army — deployed overseas right now.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether either spouse is an active service member
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: We're both civilians working regular jobs here in town.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: We're both civilians with regular jobs here.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 252's core fact: SCRA and jurisdiction rules make military status decisive; users rarely lead with it.

scenarios.opening_query: My wife and I are separating and I need to understand my rights before anything gets filed.
scenarios.hidden_fact: I'm in the Navy and about to receive PCS orders across the country, which affects where and when we can file.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I'm in the Navy with PCS orders coming — that changes where and when we can even file.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether military service and relocation orders affect the filing
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Neither of us has any military connection; we both work at the hospital.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No military involved — we both work at the hospital.
scenarios.vetting_note: Service member's own status disclosed naturally; jurisdiction complexity is the taxonomy's stated reason for the label.

scenarios.opening_query: I want out of my marriage. It's only been eight months and I already know it was a mistake.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || Family Law > Birth Certificate/Name Change
scenarios.hidden_fact: We just learned the marriage was never legally completed — the officiant was unregistered and no license was ever filed. Mostly I need my legal name changed back.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Turns out we were never legally married — unregistered officiant, no license filed. What I really need is my name changed back.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a valid legal marriage exists and what relief the user actually needs
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The marriage is legally valid — license filed and all — so it needs a real dissolution.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The marriage is fully legal — license and all — so it needs a real dissolution.
scenarios.vetting_note: Void-marriage discovery reduces the ask to a name-change petition; unusual but drawn from real intake patterns and legally coherent.

scenarios.opening_query: My ex and I divorced two years ago and the financial arrangements just don't work anymore.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || Family Law > Spousal Support
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's the alimony — I lost my job and can't pay the ordered spousal support, so I need it modified.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's the alimony — I lost my job and need the spousal support order modified.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's the child support — my ex got a big promotion and the amount should be recalculated.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's the child support — my ex's income doubled and it should be recalculated.
scenarios.vetting_note: Post-decree 'financial arrangements' splits into two exact labels depending on which order is at issue.

scenarios.opening_query: We're divorcing and the biggest fight is going to be over the retirement money.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || Family Law > QDROs
scenarios.hidden_fact: The decree was actually finalized last month — what's left is dividing his 401(k) and PERS account, which apparently needs its own court order.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The divorce is final as of last month — what's left is splitting his 401(k) and PERS, which needs its own order.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the divorce is final and only the retirement-division order remains
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Nothing is filed yet — the whole divorce still has to happen, retirement included.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Nothing's filed yet — the whole divorce is still ahead of us.
scenarios.vetting_note: QDRO work is post-decree; the finalized-decree fact isolates it from general divorce.

scenarios.opening_query: I'm ending my marriage and I'm worried about what happens with the house and my stepkids.
scenarios.final: Family Law > Same Sex Couples and Parents
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || Family Law > Same Sex Couples and Parents
scenarios.hidden_fact: My wife and I married before our state recognized it and registered a domestic partnership first — the kids were born into the partnership, and I never did a formal adoption.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: We're a same-sex couple — domestic partnership first, then marriage — and the kids were born into the partnership without me formally adopting.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether partnership-era parentage and recognition issues complicate the dissolution
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's a straightforward marriage; the kids are legally both of ours from birth.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Standard marriage — the kids are legally both of ours.
scenarios.vetting_note: Partnership-to-marriage parentage gaps are the taxonomy's reason for this label; disclosure is factual, not identity-as-trick.

scenarios.opening_query: My husband filed for divorce and I just got served. I need someone on my side fast.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || International Law > Family Law
scenarios.hidden_fact: He filed in Japan where he's stationed, our kids are with me here, and I don't know which country's courts control.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He filed in Japan where he's stationed — the kids are with me here, and I don't know which country's court controls.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the divorce involves foreign filings or cross-border custody
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: He filed at the courthouse downtown; we've both always lived here.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He filed downtown — we've always lived here.
scenarios.vetting_note: Foreign-filing fact reroutes to international family law; grounded in row 252's cross-jurisdiction confusion.

scenarios.opening_query: I want a divorce but my spouse says I'll be 'ruined' if I leave. I need to know where I stand.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || Family Law > Prenuptial Agreements
scenarios.hidden_fact: There's a prenup I signed two days before the wedding without a lawyer, and the 'ruined' threat is based on it — I need it reviewed and challenged.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: There's a prenup I signed two days before the wedding with no lawyer — that's the 'ruined' threat. I want it challenged.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a premarital agreement governs and needs review or challenge
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: There's no agreement of any kind — it's an ordinary property split he's dramatizing.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No agreement exists — he's just dramatizing an ordinary split.
scenarios.vetting_note: Prenup existence is the fact; duress-timing detail makes challenge realistic.

scenarios.opening_query: My spouse and I need to formalize our separation but we actually agree on almost everything.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || Family Law > Collaborative Family Law
scenarios.hidden_fact: We've both committed to the collaborative process — we each want counsel trained in it and have agreed in writing not to litigate.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: We're committed to the collaborative process — both want collaborative-trained counsel and signed an agreement not to litigate.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the couple has chosen the collaborative-law process
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: We just want the standard filing done cheaply; no special process.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Just the standard filing, as cheaply as possible.
scenarios.vetting_note: Process-choice disclosure maps to a dedicated taxonomy label; realistic for amicable splits.

scenarios.opening_query: I'm leaving my marriage and I have no income of my own right now. What am I entitled to?
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || Family Law > Spousal Support
scenarios.hidden_fact: I gave up my nursing career for 12 years to raise our kids while he built his practice — long-term support is the entire fight.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I gave up nursing for 12 years to raise the kids while he built his practice — support is going to be the whole fight.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether compensatory/maintenance support is the central issue
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: We earn about the same and have no kids — it's really just dividing the house and accounts.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: We earn about the same, no kids — it's just the house and accounts.
scenarios.vetting_note: Career-sacrifice disclosure elevates support from incident to core issue per the taxonomy's compensatory language.

scenarios.opening_query: Divorce is coming and I'm scared about the house because only his name is on everything.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || Family Law > Document Review
scenarios.hidden_fact: He already had his lawyer draw up a full settlement agreement and is pressuring me to sign this week — I need someone to review it before I do.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: His lawyer already drafted a settlement agreement and he wants it signed this week — I need it reviewed first.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a drafted agreement awaits review versus a case needing filing
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Nothing's been drafted — I need someone to start the whole case for me.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Nothing's drafted — I need someone to handle the whole case.
scenarios.vetting_note: Review-vs-represent is a real service distinction with its own label; the pending-document fact selects it.

scenarios.opening_query: I need help with my divorce case. My husband enlisted in the Navy right after we married.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > Military || Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.)
scenarios.hidden_fact: He washed out of training and was discharged months ago — he's a civilian again, living two towns over.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He actually washed out and was discharged months ago — he's a civilian living two towns over.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the spouse is still in military service
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: He's still in — stationed in San Diego and claiming that state protects him from our case.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He's still in, stationed in San Diego, and says that protects him from our case.
scenarios.vetting_note: Reverse direction of row 252: the discharge fact removes the military overlay.
